briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to Approve Housing Agreement for 388-Unit Rental Development at 6616 Telford Avenue
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw (Bylaw No. 14790) that would authorize a formal housing agreement with the developer of a new rental building at 6616 Telford Avenue in Burnaby. If approved, the agreement would lock in rules for 304 below-market rental units and 84 market-rate rental units — setting rent limits, tenant eligibility requirements, and long-term affordability conditions that would run with the property permanently.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if you manage or own rental property in Burnaby, this sets a precedent for affordability requirements.
briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Construction & Development, Tenant

briefs.title: Council to vote on: Bosa Properties housing agreement for 94 non-market rentals at 5980 Kathleen Ave
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would authorize a formal housing agreement with Bosa Properties (Metrotown) Inc. for a 35-storey rental building at 5980 Kathleen Avenue in Burnaby. If approved, the agreement would lock in 94 below-market rental units — 92 reserved for tenants displaced from prior sites, and 2 rented at rates at least 20% below the local market median — with these affordability rules running in perpetuity.
briefs.action_required: If you are a displaced tenant from a prior Bosa site, watch for your right-of-first-refusal notice.
briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant, Construction & Development

briefs.title: Council to vote on final approval of purpose-built rental townhouse rezoning in Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on the final adoption of a rezoning bylaw (Bylaw 14590) that would officially change the land-use rules for several properties on Bevan Street and Marine Drive in the Edmonds area. The rezoning, proposed by Mosaic Avenue Developments, would allow a purpose-built rental townhouse project called 'Bevan Townhomes' to move forward on what is a City-owned, non-market housing site.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this rezoning could affect nearby property values and rental housing supply.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Bylaw 14590 rezoning final adoption — council vote date

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Council to vote on: Rezoning approval for Bevan Townhomes development in Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on final adoption of a bylaw that would rezone a parcel of land to allow the 'Bevan Townhomes' project, a townhouse development designed by Ekistics Architecture. The bylaw has already passed three readings and a public hearing — Council must now give final approval (called 'adoption') to make the rezoning official.
briefs.action_required: Check if your property or business is near this rezoning area — construction activity may affect you.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 23, 2023 — final adoption vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on Burnaby's proposed 2026–2030 Five-Year Financial Plan
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would adopt the city's five-year financial plan covering 2026 to 2030. If approved, the plan would set out how the city expects to raise and spend money each year — starting with roughly $873 million in revenue and $1.35 billion in total spending in 2026, with property taxes making up the largest share (about 45%) of revenue.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — property tax and business tax rates in this plan could affect your costs.
briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Construction & Development, Retail

briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning of 1030 Sperling Ave in Burnaby for 'Sperling Court' Development
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw (No. 14791) that would rezone the property at 1030 Sperling Ave to allow a new development called 'Sperling Court,' designed by Kennith Kim Architecture Inc. If approved, any construction on this site must follow the approved Comprehensive Development Plan — a detailed blueprint that sets out exactly how the property can be built and used.
briefs.action_required: Monitor the Burnaby Council meeting agenda for the vote date if this development affects your business or property.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on 10 outstanding zoning and highway bylaws
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a package of 10 outstanding bylaws from 2024 that are still working through the approval process. Most are proposed changes to Burnaby's Zoning Bylaw — the rules that control what can be built and where — covering new rental housing, mixed-use developments, and one highway closure. None of these bylaws have been fully adopted yet.
briefs.action_required: Check if any listed address is near your business — a zoning change could affect your neighbourhood or property.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2025 Bylaw Tracking Index — Key Decisions Still In Progress
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on several bylaws still marked 'In Progress' as of January 2026, including zoning changes for major new housing and mixed-use developments, an updated Official Community Plan to guide growth for the next 50 years, and new rules around liquor and cannabis store spacing. A number of these proposed bylaws would directly affect construction, property development, retail licensing, and housing in Burnaby.
briefs.action_required: Review the bylaws marked 'In Progress' and attend any upcoming Council meetings if your business or property is affected.

briefs.title: Council to Consider 30+ Outstanding Burnaby Resolutions: Development, Safety & More
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review a list of over 30 unfinished resolutions from 2024, covering topics like new housing developments, fire safety bylaw updates, water metering, earthquake preparedness, and anti-racism planning. These items are still in progress or not yet started — none are finalized — and Council will consider next steps for each as work continues into 2025 and 2026.
briefs.action_required: Track specific items affecting your business — several rezoning and bylaw decisions are still pending Council approval.

briefs.title: Council to Consider 2025 Open Resolutions Index: Key Decisions Still in Progress
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review its 2025 Open Council Resolutions Index, which tracks dozens of decisions made throughout 2025 that are still being acted on. Items still in progress include major rezoning applications, a 'Buy Local' procurement policy in response to U.S. tariffs, an electric bike-share pilot program, zoning rule changes for small-scale housing, construction waste bylaw amendments, and a new language translation program — many of which could directly affect local business owners.
briefs.action_required: Review the resolutions list for items affecting your industry and watch for upcoming Council reports in early 2026.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby's 2025 Resolutions and Bylaw Progress Report
briefs.summary: At its January 27, 2026 meeting, Burnaby City Council is set to receive a status update on all open resolutions (formal decisions directing city staff to act) and bylaws (local laws) from 2025, plus any unfinished items from 2024. Council will also consider a change to how often these updates are shared — moving from four times a year to twice a year starting in 2026.
briefs.action_required: No direct action needed — monitor Burnaby Council updates twice yearly for bylaw changes affecting your business.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Approving Burnaby's 2026 Child Care Resource Group Workplan
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on endorsing the 2026 workplan for the Child Care Resources Group (CCRG) — an advisory body that guides the City on child care matters. Council will also consider approving $3,000 in funding to support a Child Care Provider Appreciation event and professional development session planned for May 2026.
briefs.action_required: If you run a child care facility in Burnaby, watch for the council vote — this workplan could affect your approval process.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Child Care Provider Appreciation Event — CCRG
Burnaby Child Care Action Plan update — scheduled start

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Anti-Racism Framework with Four Key Action Areas
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to consider adopting a proposed Anti-Racism Framework developed by the Social Planning Committee's Anti-Racism Sub-Committee. The framework would commit the City to action in four areas: Leadership & Governance, Learning & Training, Belonging & Inclusion, and Engagement & Evaluation — with the goal of making Burnaby's programs, policies, and civic spaces more equitable for racialized residents.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this framework could shape City hiring, programs, and public engagement affecting your business.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Anti-Palestinian Racism Framework as Reference Document
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider adopting a report by the Arab Canadian Lawyers Association (ACLA) as a reference document. The report defines and describes anti-Palestinian racism (APR) — a form of discrimination that silences, excludes, or dehumanizes Palestinians and those who support Palestinian rights — and proposes a framework for institutions, employers, and educators to recognize and address it.
briefs.action_required: Review the proposed framework to understand how it may affect your workplace anti-racism or equity policies.

briefs.title: Council to vote on adding anti-Palestinian racism to Burnaby's Anti-Racism Framework
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on whether to officially add a definition of anti-Palestinian racism (APR) to the city's existing Anti-Racism Framework. The proposed definition — developed by the Arab Canadian Lawyers Association — describes APR as a form of discrimination that silences, erases, or dehumanizes Palestinians and their stories.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote on January 27, 2026 — this shapes Burnaby's official anti-racism policy.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Anti-Racism Framework — APR definition council vote

briefs.title: Council to consider: Renewing GFL Environmental Inc. waste contract for $240,000
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a third renewal of its contract with GFL Environmental Inc. for waste-related services. If approved, the renewal would run from February 1, 2026 to January 31, 2027 at a cost of $240,000, bringing the total estimated contract value — including all previous terms and changes — to approximately $1,080,500.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision if your business uses or competes in waste/environmental services in Burnaby.

key_dates.label: GFL Environmental contract 3rd renewal start date — Burnaby
GFL Environmental contract 3rd renewal end date — Burnaby

briefs.title: Council to vote on $240,000 contract renewal for recyclable materials hauling in Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on renewing a contract with GFL Environmental Inc. to haul recyclable materials from City facilities to an approved recycling facility. The proposed one-year renewal would run from February 1, 2026 to January 31, 2027, at an estimated cost of $240,000 including taxes.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect recycling service contracts in Burnaby.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Proposed $123,475 Contract Change for ADS Environmental Technologies
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract change — meaning an agreed adjustment to an existing contract's price — with ADS Environmental Technologies Inc. The change would add $123,475 to the existing agreement, bringing the total estimated contract value to $651,606 including tax. This represents a 23.3% increase over the first two years of the contract.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on January 27, 2026 — this contract change is pending approval.

briefs.title: Council to vote on $123,475 contract change for Burnaby sewer water level sensors
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving a contract change worth $123,475 (including taxes) with ADS Environmental Technologies Inc. This would cover 45 replacement battery packs, 20 high-gain antennas, and 12 months of wireless monitoring for the city's 45 sewer water level sensors — equipment that detects blockages and prevents sewer overflows in real time.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on January 27, 2026 — this is a city infrastructure contract with no direct action required from most businesses.

briefs.title: Council to vote on $1M contract for Burnaby Village Museum meadow and plaza upgrades
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a construction contract worth approximately $1,039,028 (including taxes) to Holaco Construction (1997) Ltd. The work would upgrade the meadow and Tram Barn Plaza at Burnaby Village Museum — adding a plaza surface, power and water hookups for food trucks and events, better drainage, lighting, and improved pathways to support year-round programming.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — food truck and event vendors may gain new opportunities at Burnaby Village Museum.

briefs.title: Council to vote on $3.8M security system upgrade for Burnaby city facilities
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a $3.8 million contract to Paladin Technologies Inc. to upgrade and unify security systems across city facilities. The five-year contract (February 2026 – January 2031) would cover new cameras, access control systems, and ongoing maintenance — consolidating separate security setups into one city-wide platform.

If approved, the contract would run from February 1, 2026 to January 31, 2027, with up to four one-year renewal options.

briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a contract worth up to $1,300,000 (including taxes) to Metro Traffic Ltd. for flagging and traffic control services. If approved, the contract would run from February 1, 2026 to January 31, 2027, with up to four one-year renewal options, covering road work, maintenance, and emergency response across the city.

briefs.title: Council to vote on $813,540 contract to replace aging air handling unit at Eileen Dailly Pool
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a contract worth $813,540 (including taxes) to Fortius Mechanical Inc. to replace a worn-out air handling unit — the system that moves and filters air — at the Eileen Dailly Leisure Pool & Fitness Centre. The unit has reached the end of its lifespan and must be replaced to keep the lobby and fitness areas properly ventilated.

briefs.title: Council to vote on $2.625M contract for Burnaby outdoor pool tank repairs
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a $2,625,000 contract to ETRO Construction Limited to manage and carry out repairs on the city's aging outdoor pool tanks. The work — starting with Central Park Outdoor Pool in 2026–2027 — would include sandblasting, concrete crack fixes, pipe relining, and recoating, with three other pools (Kensington Park, MacPherson Park, and Robert Burnaby) scheduled in later phases.
briefs.action_required: If your construction firm does similar work, watch for future RFPs as later pool phases are still unfunded.

briefs.title: Council to vote on $5.75M contract for Burnaby fire station infrastructure upgrades
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a $5.75 million contract (including taxes) to Holaco Construction (1997) Ltd. to upgrade emergency infrastructure at Fire Stations No. 1 and No. 2. The work would replace aging underground fuel tanks with above-ground systems and install new emergency generators — keeping both stations fully operational during construction.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision if you are a construction contractor — this contract has been awarded pending approval.

Burnaby 2026–2030 Financial Plan approval — funding for this project

briefs.title: Council to vote on cost-sharing agreement with Burnaby School District 41 for 2026 election
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on allowing the City's Chief Election Officer to sign a cost-sharing agreement with Burnaby School District 41. Under this proposed deal, the City would run the School Trustee election on the School District's behalf — as it has done in past elections — with the School District pitching in by providing school buildings as voting locations and staff time to help offset costs.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this affects how the October 2026 local election is run in Burnaby.

key_dates.label: Burnaby 2026 General Local Election day

briefs.title: Council to Consider: BC Speculation and Vacancy Tax 2024 Results and Upcoming Rate Increases
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review a technical briefing on BC's Speculation and Vacancy Tax (SVT) — a yearly tax on homes that are left empty or owned by people who don't pay income tax in BC. The briefing covers 2024 results and flags upcoming changes: starting January 2026, tax rates will rise (from 0.5% to 1% for most Canadians, and from 2% to 3% for foreign owners), while the BC resident tax credit will double from $2,000 to $4,000.
briefs.action_required: If you own residential property in BC, check whether the new 2026 SVT rates affect your tax bill.

key_dates.label: BC Speculation and Vacancy Tax rate increases take effect
BC SVT resident tax credit increase to $4,000 takes effect

briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Finance & Insurance, Construction & Development

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Mayor Urges Province to Reform Speculation and Vacancy Tax Funding
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Mayor has written to BC's Minister of Finance asking the province to change how Speculation and Vacancy Tax (SVT) money is shared — the SVT is a provincial tax on homes left empty or underused. The letter asks that a portion of SVT funds go directly to cities like Burnaby, and that Burnaby be given the authority to run its own Empty Homes Tax, similar to Vancouver's.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Province's response — changes to SVT funding or a new Burnaby Empty Homes Tax could affect property owners.
briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Construction & Development, Finance & Insurance

briefs.title: Council to Consider Sending Feedback on BC's Speculation and Vacancy Tax
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on submitting a formal response to the BC Ministry of Finance about the Speculation and Vacancy Tax (SVT) — a provincial tax on empty or underused homes. The proposed response would renew Burnaby's push to receive a direct share of SVT revenue for local housing projects, and to gain the power to create its own Empty Homes Tax, similar to Vancouver.
briefs.action_required: If you own vacant or investment property in Burnaby, note that SVT rates are rising in 2026.

key_dates.label: BC Speculation and Vacancy Tax rate increases take effect

briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Finance & Insurance, Construction & Development

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to Guarantee $95M Loan for Edmonds Housing Development
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would allow the City of Burnaby to guarantee a $95-million loan for the Burnaby Housing Authority (BHA) — a city-owned corporation — to build housing near Marine Drive and Bevan Street. A loan guarantee means the City promises to repay the debt if the BHA cannot. The City would also be authorized to borrow up to $95 million itself, if needed, to cover that guarantee, with a repayment term of up to 30 years.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this large public housing project may affect local construction and property markets.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Finance & Insurance

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to Guarantee $100M Loan for New Housing Development at 10th and Newcombe
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would allow the City of Burnaby to guarantee — meaning co-sign and back with city funds — a loan of up to $100 million taken out by the Burnaby Housing Authority (BHA). The money would fund a new housing development at 10th and Newcombe, and the city could borrow up to $100 million itself, repayable over up to 30 years, if the BHA cannot repay the loan.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this large public housing commitment may affect local construction and property markets.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Finance & Insurance

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on $195M loan guarantee bylaws for affordable housing
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on two proposed bylaws that would allow the City of Burnaby to back — that is, guarantee repayment of — loans totalling $195M for the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation. The loans, sought through a federal housing program, would fund 386 non-market housing units at two sites. The bylaws are a precaution: they give the City legal authority to borrow if the loans ever go unpaid, though staff say they do not expect that to happen.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if passed, these guarantees will reduce Burnaby's future borrowing room.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Loan Authorization Bylaws — Inspector of Municipalities approval window opens

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Finance & Insurance

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Proposed Bike Learning Zone for Children in Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider findings from a public engagement report on a proposed Bike Learning Zone — a car-free space where children can learn to ride bikes and practice road safety. The report shows strong community support, and its findings may be used to shape funding and planning recommendations that Council will review before any final decision is made.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council decision — it could create opportunities for local businesses to partner or supply the new facility.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Site Selection for Burnaby's New Bike Learning Zone
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider a report recommending the best location for a new Bike Learning Zone (BLZ) — a dedicated area where children and youth can safely learn to ride bikes. A scoring table comparing six candidate sites has been prepared, with the site at 7679 18th Street receiving the highest score (4.9 out of 5) based on factors like proximity to cycling routes, neighbourhood need, and site availability.
briefs.action_required: If your business is near any of the six candidate sites, watch for the council decision — it may affect nearby traffic and land use.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Proposed Bike Learning Zone Location in Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider a proposed Bike Learning Zone covering approximately 850 square metres at a location identified in a city map dated January 2026. The proposal is still under review and has not yet been approved — no final decision has been made on whether this zone will be established.
briefs.action_required: Check the Burnaby Council meeting agenda for the vote date if this zone could affect your business location.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Approving Burnaby Bike Learning Zone Location and Grant Funding
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving 7679 18th Street (in the Edmonds area) as the site for a new bike learning zone — an outdoor facility with cycling paths, traffic signs, and intersection markings to teach road safety. Council will also consider directing staff to apply for outside grant funding to help pay for the design and construction, which is estimated at roughly $350,000.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — it could affect grant timelines and construction activity near 7679 18th Street.

key_dates.label: ICBC Community Grants Program — Burnaby bike learning zone application window closes

briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning of 5227 Lane Street, Burnaby — Council to Consider
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider a rezoning application (reference #19-53214) for the property at 5227 Lane Street. Rezoning means changing the official land-use rules for that address — in this case, potentially allowing a different type of building or business activity than what is currently permitted under the existing zoning designations shown on the site sketch.
briefs.action_required: Monitor Burnaby Council meeting agendas for the public hearing date on this rezoning to have your say.

briefs.title: Council to vote on converting 35 affordable rental units to market-rate at 5227 Lane Street
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a developer's request to convert 35 below-market rental units (priced at CMHC median rates — a federally set affordability benchmark) into full market-rate rentals at a new six-storey building at 5227 Lane Street. The developer says rising construction costs and financing charges have made the project unworkable under the current affordability rules, and that removing them is needed for the project to move forward.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the January 27, 2026 council decision — it could affect rental supply and rates in the Royal Oak area.

briefs.title: Council to vote on: Burnaby's proposed 2026–2030 Financial Plan and budget
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on adopting the proposed 2026–2030 Financial Plan, which would set the city's total spending at roughly $1.36 billion for 2026. The plan would introduce a 2.9% property tax increase (about $61 more per year for an average home), a continued 1.9% Growth Infrastructure Investment Levy, no increase to water rates, and a 3.5% sewer rate increase — while funding major projects like the Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre, Cameron Community Centre, and a new Community Safety Building.
briefs.action_required: Check how the proposed tax and utility rate changes affect your business costs before the February 10 adoption vote.

briefs.title: Council to vote on Burnaby's 2026–2030 Financial Plan, including 2.9% property tax increase
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on the 2026–2030 Financial Plan and a matching five-year budget bylaw (a bylaw is a local law passed by a city). If approved, the plan would raise property taxes by 2.9%, keep water rates flat at 0.0%, increase sewer rates by 3.5%, and maintain a 1.9% Growth Infrastructure Levy — a charge that helps pay for infrastructure needed as the city grows.

briefs.title: Council to Consider New Non-Residential Zoning Districts Under Burnaby 2050 Plan
briefs.summary: At its January 13, 2026 meeting, Burnaby City Council reviewed several proposals with potential impacts on local businesses. Most notably, a proposed zoning bylaw rewrite would create five new non-residential zoning districts as part of the Burnaby 2050 land use plan — but Council sent it back to staff for more information after raising concerns about setbacks, unclear use definitions, and restrictions on recycling facilities.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the revised zoning bylaw report — it could affect where and how your business can operate in Burnaby.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail
